visual_attributes.morphology: Aye-ayes have a slender body with a long bushy tail, large ears, and rodent-like incisors.
distinctive_features: Thin, elongated middle finger used for foraging
Large, sensitive ears with complex ridges
visual_attributes.texture: Thick fur with a coarse texture
visual_attributes.size_and_scale: Approximately 60 cm in length with a tail longer than its body, weighing around 2 kg
visual_attributes.structural_arrangement: The body is covered in thick fur with a bushy tail, and the head features large ears and prominent eyes.

domain_specific_visual.FINGER_STRUCTURE: Ball-and-socket joint in the third finger
domain_specific_visual.EAR_GEOMETRY: Ridges on the inner surface for sound focusing

functional_attributes.primary_function: Nocturnal foraging for grubs and larvae using percussive foraging

domain_specific_functional.ECOLOGICAL_NICHE: Fills the niche of a woodpecker by extracting invertebrates from wood
domain_specific_functional.REPRODUCTION: Females give birth every two to three years

relationships.description: The aye-aye is native to Madagascar.
A related species that became extinct within the last 1000 years.

contextual_attributes.cultural_significance: Considered a magical animal in Malagasy culture
